title: Policy Constellation Map
description: Fields and institutions as a single constellation. Selecting a point reveals how influence, information, and funding move through the landscape.
note: Illustrative links, drawn from the cited sources.

nodes.label: AI Governance
nodes.description: A policy field where model capability, risk management, legal accountability, and international rule-making meet.

nodes.label: Compute Governance
nodes.description: The governance questions created by concentrated computing capacity, advanced chips, and cloud infrastructure.

nodes.label: Cybersecurity
nodes.description: A security field increasingly linked to model misuse, critical infrastructure, and strategic competition.

nodes.label: Military AI
nodes.description: The point where autonomy, deterrence, procurement, legal debate, and technical capability converge.

nodes.label: Semiconductor Policy
nodes.description: Manufacturing capacity, supply chains, workforce, and national competitiveness becoming one policy system.

nodes.label: Export Controls
nodes.description: A policy lever that links strategic technology, trade, security, and access to advanced hardware.

nodes.label: Arctic Security
nodes.description: Climate, maritime security, critical minerals, and undersea infrastructure widening into one field.

nodes.label: Undersea Infrastructure
nodes.description: Cables and pipelines whose strategic importance surfaces mainly when a disruption makes them visible.

nodes.label: NIST
nodes.description: A standards body turning technical language into frameworks that later shape regulation.

nodes.label: OECD
nodes.description: An international organization convening actors around mission-oriented, cross-sector problems.

nodes.label: EU Commission
nodes.description: A regulator whose risk-based AI framework sets a reference point for other jurisdictions.

nodes.label: NATO DIANA
nodes.description: An accelerator connecting emerging and disruptive technology with collective defence needs.

nodes.label: Standards Bodies
nodes.description: Organizations that make technical practice interoperable and durable enough to influence policy.

edges.explanation: Standards work translates broad governance aims into durable technical practice.
Shared principles spread a common governance vocabulary across countries.
Model governance depends on where computing capacity is built and controlled.
Security risks shape how AI systems are evaluated, deployed, and governed.
A risk-based legal framework anchors much of the governance conversation.
Civilian governance and defence applications share technical and legal questions.
Standards give governance aims a more durable technical and organizational form.
Access to advanced hardware ties governance to trade and security policy.
Computing capacity and chip manufacturing form one strategic supply question.
Infrastructure is already shaped by policy on strategic technology flows.
Chip strategy and export policy pull on the same supply chains.
Public programs link manufacturing with research, workforce, and resilience.
Defence accelerators fund the bridge from emerging technology to capability.
Strategic competition connects defence applications to hardware access.
Risk frameworks give organizations practical footing for managing threats.
Interoperable practice hardens security expectations into norms.
Northern defence priorities pull emerging technology toward the region.
Security in the North increasingly means protecting undersea systems.
Resilience of undersea systems draws defence-innovation attention.
Cross-sector convening frames the Arctic as more than a regional issue.
International principles and standards reinforce one another.
Regulation leans on standards to become operational.
